# Secure and Reliable Systems

A design-time harness distilled from Google's *Building Secure and Reliable
Systems* (SRS). It changes how you design and review systems, not what facts you
can recite. The book is ~700 pages; this skill is the part that alters decisions.

## The one idea everything hangs on

Reliability and security are the same question asked about two different
adversaries: **does the system keep doing the right thing when conditions turn
hostile?**

- Reliability's hostility is *indifferent* — hardware dies, traffic spikes, a
  dependency times out, a bug ships. It doesn't adapt to your defenses.
- Security's hostility is *intelligent* — an adversary who wants the system to
  fail, watches what you do, and moves to wherever you're weakest.

Because the failure shape is shared, the defenses overlap: defense in depth,
blast-radius containment, graceful degradation, fast recovery, honest
observability serve both. Because the intent differs, the two also **trade off
against each other and against features and cost.** A security control can wreck
reliability (an aggressive rate limiter drops good traffic; a locked-down
recovery path is one you can't use at 3 a.m.). A reliability convenience can open
an attack (a frictionless failover is also a frictionless pivot for an attacker).

So the whole method is: **design for both at once, and wherever they conflict,
make the tradeoff explicit and reversible instead of silently picking one.** A
design that optimizes one axis and never names what it cost on the others is the
thing to distrust.

## How to use this skill

**When designing** — before committing to an architecture, walk the eight lenses
below. For each, ask its core question against the system in front of you. Most
lenses will be a quick "handled" or "not applicable"; one or two will force a
real change. Record the security-vs-reliability tradeoffs you hit as decisions,
not as afterthoughts (an ADR is the natural home).

**When reviewing** — run the same eight lenses as a checklist against the design,
PR, or ADR. Produce findings ranked by **blast radius × likelihood**, not by how
easy they were to spot. Say what you checked and found clean, not only what's
wrong. Hand line-by-line vulnerability hunting on the actual diff to the
`security-review` skill; this skill judges the *shape* of the system.

**When neither applies** — skip it. A pure algorithm, a throwaway script, a
system with no adversary and no availability requirement doesn't need this. Don't
perform the ritual where it buys nothing.

## The eight lenses

Each lens has a core question and a pointer to its reference file. Read the
reference file when a lens is clearly load-bearing for the system at hand and you
need the concrete patterns, not before. The one-liners here are enough to know
whether a lens bites.

1. **Least privilege** — *Who can do what, for how long, and why that much?*
   Minimum authority, shortest lifetime, smallest trusted computing base.
   Zero standing privilege, just-in-time grants, multi-party authorization for
   dangerous actions, audited break-glass. → `references/least-privilege.md`

2. **Understandability** — *Can you state this system's invariants and trust
   boundaries? If not, you can't defend them.* Narrow interfaces, identifiable
   trust boundaries, the safe path as the default and the unsafe path as the
   hard one. → `references/understandability.md`

3. **Changing landscape** — *When you must change any component fast under
   pressure, can you?* Small, frequent, reversible changes; routine secret and
   credential rotation; no flag-day migrations; patchable dependencies.
   → `references/changing-landscape.md`

4. **Resilience** — *When a control or component fails, does the system degrade
   or collapse — and does it fail safe or fail open, on purpose?* Layered
   defenses where none is solely load-bearing, deliberate failure modes,
   compartments that cap blast radius. → `references/resilience.md`

5. **Recovery** — *After compromise or failure, how fast and how safely do you
   get back to a known-good state — and have you rehearsed it?* Rollback and
   undo, a trusted baseline, rate-limited recovery actions, not trusting maybe-
   compromised components mid-recovery. → `references/recovery.md`

6. **DoS mitigation** — *Who pays more when load turns hostile, you or the
   attacker?* Push cost onto the attacker, degrade gracefully, isolate so one
   tenant can't starve the rest, defend at the right layer, kill amplification.
   → `references/dos.md`

7. **Supply chain & deployment** — *Is the code that runs the code you
   reviewed?* Artifact provenance and binary authorization, code review as a
   security control, a trustworthy build/CI chain, config governed like code.
   → `references/supply-chain.md`

8. **Crisis & culture** — *When it goes wrong for real, who's in charge, and does
   the org make the safe path the easy path?* Incident command, blameless
   review, rehearsed disaster plans, aligned incentives. → `references/crisis-culture.md`

## The tradeoff move (the part people skip)

When two lenses pull against each other, don't resolve it in your head and move
on. Name it:

> *"Failing closed here protects tenancy but takes the service down if the policy
> engine is unreachable; failing open keeps serving but lets unvetted workloads
> through. Choosing fail-closed because a tenancy breach is unrecoverable and an
> outage is; adding a cached-last-known-good verdict so an engine blip doesn't
> mean an outage."*

That sentence — the conflict, the choice, the reason, the mitigation — is the
actual output of this skill. If a design or review never produces one, either the
system is genuinely trivial or a tradeoff got buried.

## Anti-patterns this skill exists to catch

- A single control doing all the work ("we have auth at the gateway") with
  nothing behind it — no defense in depth.
- Standing admin credentials, shared service accounts, human-held cluster keys —
  privilege that never expires and can't be attributed.
- A recovery path nobody has ever run, discovered to be broken during the first
  real incident.
- "Secure" and "reliable" claimed on the same design with no acknowledgment of
  where they fought — usually meaning one silently lost.
- Blast radius unbounded: one tenant, one region, one bad deploy takes everything
  with it.
- The safe way being the hard way, so operators route around it under pressure.

## Findings

**[RECOVERY / SUPPLY CHAIN] Live ODCR apply runs on local state** — `runbooks/deploy-platform.md:57` / blast radius: region + state trust-root / likelihood: med — The bootstrap step for the single most irreversible module reads `terraform init -backend=false` with the inline comment `# wire real backend for a live apply`. The flag does the opposite of the comment: `-backend=false` skips backend init, so the following `terraform apply -var-file=held.tfvars` (which creates the `prevent_destroy` held reservations) writes **local** state — no DynamoDB lock, no S3 versioning, no shared source of truth. This directly contradicts `backend.tf.example:39-41`, which singles out this exact module: "the ODCR module especially must never race (capacity is irreversible)." A lost/laptop-local state file orphans reservations that carry `end_date_type = unlimited` + `prevent_destroy`. Fix: copy `backend.tf` into the odcr dir (unique key) and `terraform init` against the real S3+Dynamo backend before any capacity-touching apply; delete the `-backend=false` line.

**[CRISIS] Break-glass is pre-authorized but not audited** — `runbooks/training-intake-break-glass.md:12-29`, `runbooks/operations.md:9,140` / blast radius: workload/region / likelihood: med — The hub-down training-intake path is well pre-authorized by condition (hub down >4h AND urgent, ADR 0004) and well-bounded ("Never: patch quota… submit inference… leave as default"). But nothing on the path requires **recording the break-glass invocation** — no event, no ledger row, no post-hoc review trigger. `operations.md:140` notes hand-applying "breaks audit," yet the sanctioned break-glass writes (spoke-direct submit; "the lending controller's stop path drains lendable nodes" at `operations.md:127`) emit no audit record of who invoked them, when, or why. Pre-authorized without audited is half the control. Fix: require every break-glass action to write a timestamped record (who/why/what) and auto-open a review.

**[CRISIS] No incident command structure or blameless posture defined** — `runbooks/gpu-node-quarantine.md:91`, `runbooks/node-scrub.md:111`, `runbooks/capacity-carve.md:76` / blast radius: org process / likelihood: med — Four runbooks say "open an incident" but no runbook or ADR defines **who runs it**: no Incident Commander role, no comms/scribe split, no severity ladder, no postmortem template, no blameless framing. The only "Roles" line in the corpus is service ownership (`service-migration.md:14`), not incident roles. `game-day.md:132` produces a "signed-off game-day report" but that is a gate artifact, not a retrospective. Fix: add an incident-response runbook defining IC/comms/scribe, escalation, and a blameless postmortem template.

**[RECOVERY / CRISIS] Suspected-compromise recovery destroys forensic evidence** — `runbooks/operations.md:33,116-120` / blast radius: cloud-account / likelihood: low — "Rotate spoke credentials… or suspected hub compromise" and "Rebuild the hub from Terraform + git (it is fully reproducible)" give a fast, clean recovery — but on a **security** incident, rebuilding the hub from git before capturing the running state wipes exactly the evidence needed to learn how it was breached. There is no "preserve evidence before you rebuild/rotate" step anywhere. Fix: add an evidence-preservation gate (snapshot instance/EBS, export audit logs, isolate before rebuild) to the compromise path, distinct from the plain availability-outage rebuild.

**[SUPPLY CHAIN / RECOVERY] Checkpoint store uses the AWS-managed KMS key with no bucket policy** — `infra/terraform/regions/pilot/checkpoint-store/main.tf:43-48` / blast radius: store / likelihood: med — `sse_algorithm = "aws:kms"` with **no `kms_master_key_id`** resolves to the account's `aws/s3` managed key, whose key policy cannot be scoped. Combined with no bucket policy (only IAM/IRSA gates access, per `outputs.tf:1-4`), any account principal holding `s3:GetObject` + the default `aws/s3` decrypt can read checkpoints. These hold arbitrary R&D training state that scrub (`node-scrub.md:5-9`) treats as an untrusted trust domain versus customer-data inference — so it deserves a key with an access-scoped policy. Fix: provision a customer-managed KMS CMK with a key policy limited to the trainer IRSA roles, set `kms_master_key_id`, and add a deny-by-default bucket policy (enforce TLS + the CMK).

**[SUPPLY CHAIN] Trust-root write access is unscoped in the provided config** — `infra/terraform/backend.tf.example:24-45`, `infra/terraform/mgmt/main.tf:22-28`, `infra/terraform/regions/pilot/main.tf:22` / blast radius: cloud-account / likelihood: low — State is the trust root: whoever writes `synorg-terraform-state` and runs `mgmt` (`enable_cluster_creator_admin_permissions = true`, granting the caller cluster-admin on the fleet-reconciling hub) effectively owns the cloud. The backend example sets encrypt+lock+versioning but nothing scopes **who may write the state bucket or assume the deploy identity** — no bucket policy, no MFA-delete, no note on least-privilege of the deploy role. The hub-admin concentration is documented as intentional bootstrap-then-break-glass (comments are honest about it), but the state-bucket access boundary is simply absent. Fix: ship a state-bucket policy + role guidance (least-privilege deploy role, MFA-delete, restrict `s3:PutObject` on the state prefix) alongside the example.

**[CHANGING LANDSCAPE / SUPPLY CHAIN] Spoke API server default admits the entire VPC /16** — `infra/terraform/regions/pilot/main.tf:29-38`, `variables.tf:85-89` / blast radius: region / likelihood: low — `hub_ingress_cidr` defaults to `10.42.0.0/16` (the disposable e2e VPC's whole range) and is fed straight into a 443 ingress rule on the spoke API server. The comment says "scope this to the hub's actual CIDR," but the wide default is the failure mode: ship the default to prod and every host in the VPC can reach the spoke control plane. Fix: make `hub_ingress_cidr` required (no default) so a prod apply must state the hub's real narrow CIDR.

**[RECOVERY] Hub-rebuild / state-restore is asserted, not rehearsed** — `runbooks/operations.md:118` / blast radius: cloud-account / likelihood: low — "Rebuild the hub from Terraform + git (it is fully reproducible)" is the stated DR path, but no runbook drills it. What *is* rehearsed is genuinely rehearsed — `game-day.md` drives real preemption, and `service-migration.md:100-103` rehearses the ALB rollback once per fleet — which makes the un-drilled hub-from-zero rebuild and state-restore the aspirational gap. Fix: add a periodic hub-rebuild + state-restore DR drill with a pass artifact, same as the game-day gate.

**[RECOVERY] Checkpoint lifecycle omits incomplete-multipart-upload cleanup** — `infra/terraform/regions/pilot/checkpoint-store/main.tf:63-76` / blast radius: store / likelihood: low — The lifecycle config expires noncurrent versions only. The design writes "multi-GB checkpoint shards per node" as a concurrent burst inside a 120 s grace (`main.tf:11-16`); shards killed mid-flush at the boundary leave orphaned incomplete multipart uploads that bill indefinitely and are invisible to the versioning fallback. Fix: add `abort_incomplete_multipart_upload { days_after_initiation = 1 }`.

## Checked and clean

- **The one source-tree IAM wildcard is justified, not a finding** — `infra/terraform/regions/pilot/main.tf:99` `resources = ["*"]` applies only to `ec2:DescribeCapacityReservations`, which AWS does not support resource-level scoping for; the *consumption* actions `ec2:RunInstances`/`ec2:CreateFleet` are scoped to the exact held-ODCR ARNs (`main.tf:105-106`), and the whole policy is gated on `count = length(var.odcr_reservation_arns) > 0` (`main.tf:91`). This is least-privilege done correctly. (All other `"*"` hits in the tree are in the vendored `.terraform/modules/karpenter` cache, out of scope.)
- **No hardcoded secrets, account IDs, or ARNs in source** — the `[0-9]{12}`/`arn:aws`/`secret`/`token` sweep over source `.tf` returned only the SSE/versioning comment lines; ODCR ARNs flow through variables/outputs (`odcr/outputs.tf:10-13`, `pilot/variables.tf:69-73`), never literals. Inline secrets are policy-denied and ESO is namespace-scoped (`deploy-platform.md:120`).
- **Providers hash-locked in all four roots** — `.terraform.lock.hcl` present under `mgmt`, `regions/pilot`, `regions/pilot/checkpoint-store`, `regions/pilot/odcr`, each pinning `hashicorp/aws 5.100.0` with `h1:` hashes (the `versions.tf` `~> 5.0` constraint is backstopped by the lock).
- **State backend hardened** — `backend.tf.example:41,43` sets a DynamoDB lock table and `encrypt = true`; versioning is documented as the rollback path (`:18`).
- **Checkpoint store S3 posture solid** — versioning Enabled (`main.tf:30-36`), all four public-access-block flags true (`:52-59`), SSE on, noncurrent-version expiry (`:63-76`), `force_destroy` left default-false, FSx tier gated off by default (`variables.tf:35-39`).
- **ODCR blast-radius discipline is excellent** — `prevent_destroy` on held reservations (`odcr/main.tf:33-35`), positive-count validation (`odcr/variables.tf:46-49`), and a `declared_instance_counts` verify-before-terminate gate (`odcr/outputs.tf:26-29`) enforced one-instance-per-invocation in `capacity-carve.md:108-114`.
- **Private-by-default control planes** — hub and spoke API endpoints private by default (`mgmt/main.tf:17-19`, `pilot/main.tf:16`), hub admin explicitly bootstrap-then-break-glass (`mgmt/main.tf:25-28`).
- **game-day is a real rehearsal, not checklist theatre** — real preemption, `repeatRuns` before any verdict, an abort-on-unmeasurable-evidence precondition (`game-day.md:6,28-37,103,144`), and a cross-run variance gate (`:109`).
- **Strangler migration is incremental with a cheap rollback** — weight-flip rollback with ECS kept warm, no data migration (`service-migration.md:90-103`).

## Security×reliability tradeoffs observed

- **Recovery vs security (NOT handled): hub rebuild erases forensics.** Rebuild-from-git is the fast availability recovery but destroys breach evidence on a compromise (`operations.md:33,118`). Named above; no evidence-preservation step exists.
- **Recovery vs security (NOT handled): local-state ODCR apply.** `deploy-platform.md:57` trades the shared-locking/versioning safety of the remote backend for a local apply on the most irreversible module. Named above.
- **Availability vs cost (handled): the $350k/month held book.** ADR 0005 names the unlimited-duration ODCR hold as availability insurance and offsets the premium with training borrow, measured first-class via `utilization-of-held` + `idle-burn` against a 70% floor with a quarterly review (`0005-...:16-28`).
- **Security vs reliability (handled): scrub-on-return.** Discarding the instance to reset the GPU trust domain (`node-scrub.md:5-9`) costs return-to-service latency; bounded by the `nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds` budget (`:104`) with warm-floor-resize-first remediation (`game-day.md:114-124`) so the security reset never gets traded away for schedule progress.
- **Availability vs security (partially handled): wide spoke-API ingress default.** `hub_ingress_cidr = 10.42.0.0/16` favors hub-reconcile reachability over tight scoping; the comment flags it but the wide default remains (`pilot/main.tf:29-38`). Named above.</result>

## Security×reliability tradeoffs observed

- **Fail-closed Kyverno admission gating all pod creation, no break-glass** (finding #5). A Kyverno outage denies every Pod fleet-wide. Named by the design as `Enforce` but the outage escape hatch is **not handled** — no cached fallback or documented bypass in scope.
- **`deny-cross-namespace-refs` VAP is `failurePolicy: Fail` bound cluster-wide** (`namespaceSelector: {}`, `policies/vap/deny-cross-namespace-refs.yaml:12,68-69`). A CEL evaluation error would deny pods in every namespace including `kube-system`. **Mostly handled** by it being a built-in in-process VAP (no webhook/network dependency), so the only trigger is a CEL bug, not an operational outage — a materially safer fail-closed than the Kyverno webhook above. The README's "binding to all namespaces costs nothing" (line 66-67) is true for latency but understates this blast radius.
- **HEAD auto-sync with `selfHeal: true` + `prune: true`** (findings #1, #4) is a reliability feature (drift auto-correction) that doubles as the fleet-wide auto-deploy blast-radius amplifier. The `observability.yaml:9` note records the inverse failure — a mis-placed Application "retried forever," a reliability outage caused by a sync-topology mistake — showing the auto-sync coupling cuts both ways. Partially handled (self-heal is deliberate); the provenance gap on the input side is not.
- **ADR 0004 accepts a hub-outage training-intake pause as the safe default** (`0004:11-16`) — a clean, explicitly-named availability-vs-simplicity tradeoff (reject hub HA until multi-region need). Handled and reasoned; the only gap is the break-glass audit/alerting (finding #12).

## Findings

**[RESILIENCE] A crash between `cordon` and `nodeclaim delete` strands a lent node that both reclaim paths then deliberately skip** — `controllers/lending/reconcile.sh:274-283` / blast: pool+workload / likelihood: med. `reclaim_node` cordons (`:274`), drains (`:276`, up to `grace*3` = 360s), then deletes the NodeClaim (`:280`) — non-atomic. The idempotency guard that makes re-fires safe is also the trap: `reconcile_taints` skips any cordoned node as an "in-flight reclaim" (`:330-331` `[ "$cordoned" != "true" ] || continue`) and `reconcile_waves` excludes cordoned nodes from selection (`:521` `select(.spec.unschedulable != true)`). Those guards are only valid while the process that cordoned the node is still alive to finish it. If the pod dies during the ~360s drain window (OOM, deploy rollover, node loss), the node comes back **cordoned + still lent + still running training**, and there is no reconciler that resumes an interrupted reclaim — both paths treat it as "someone else is handling it." It sits lent past the ramp; only the warm floor saves inference. The once-per-occurrence marker is touched *before* the loop (`:517`), so the wave won't re-fire either. Fix direction: on each tick, actively re-drive cordoned-lent nodes toward completion (re-issue drain + NodeClaim-delete) rather than skipping them; distinguish "cordoned by a live in-flight reclaim" from "cordoned and abandoned" via a timestamp/annotation.

**[RESILIENCE] The warm floor is not isolated from the reclaimer — a single schedule field aims the full cordon/drain/terminate machinery, and schedule PRs ride the auto-approved lane** — `clusters/pilot/lending/lending-controller.yaml:61-70`, `clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml:43` / blast: fleet (the inference floor itself) / likelihood: low, consequence catastrophic. The ClusterRole grants `nodes` patch/update and `nodeclaims` delete **cluster-wide, unrestricted** (`:61-63`, `:68-70`). The only thing keeping reclaim off the warm floor is the code selector `karpenter.sh/nodepool=$pool` where `pool` = `targets.lendablePool` read straight from the schedule (`reconcile.sh:306,:375,:520`). Warm floor and lendable share the same EC2NodeClass, instance types, and ODCR pool (`nodepool-gpu-warm-floor.yaml:39-45` vs `nodepool-gpu-lendable.yaml:29-35`) — they differ only by label/taint. `validate_schedule` never checks that `lendablePool` is actually the lendable pool. A schedule setting `lendablePool: gpu-warm-floor` would cordon+drain+delete-NodeClaim the never-lent inference floor — and the floor's protections (balloon + `budgets: nodes:"0"`, `nodepool-gpu-warm-floor.yaml:50-51`) only stop *Karpenter* voluntary consolidation, not an external NodeClaim delete. Worse, the evidence plane targets an 80% auto-approval share for PRs (`recording-rules.yaml:114-122`, `slo-definitions.yaml:98-104`), and the schedule ConfigMap is "the ONLY write path" (`schedule.yaml:1-5`) — so a malformed pool value could merge without human review. Fix direction: pin the pool selector to a constant (or add a validate_schedule assertion that `lendablePool != gpu-warm-floor`), and require human approval on schedule.yaml diffs.

**[CHANGING LANDSCAPE] No fast kill switch and no abort — the only lending write path is a git PR, and the one way to "stop now" triggers an un-staged mass reclaim** — `clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml:1-8`, `controllers/lending/reconcile.sh:318-344` / blast: fleet / likelihood: med. There is no `lendingEnabled: false` flag and no break-glass. To stop lending under incident you either open a PR (CI + merge + ArgoCD sync — not seconds-fast) or scale the Deployment to 0, which *freezes* state (cordoned nodes stay cordoned, drained borrowers stay `spec.active=false` until phase exit). And narrowing the window to force a stop is not gentle: `reconcile_taints` on close iterates **every** still-lent un-cordoned node and calls `reclaim_node` on each in one tick with no `reclaimFraction` staging (`:318-344`) — the staged waves (`schedule.yaml:82-96`) apply only to scheduled reclaim, not to a window-close reclaim. So "close the window now" = the `storm-all-at-once` case, absorbed only because the warm floor backstops it (`scenarios.yaml:96-115`). The changing-landscape lens wants a *governed fast path*; a git round-trip is governed but not fast. Fix direction: a controller-honored pause flag (env or a dedicated ConfigMap key) that halts new actuation without freezing recovery, plus a documented emergency abort.

**[DoS] No per-tenant quota — every training team shares one `training-borrow` ClusterQueue, so one team can consume the entire borrow limit** — `clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml:41-60`, `clusters/pilot/kueue/localqueue-team-example.yaml:20-26` / blast: workload (other training tenants; inference unaffected) / likelihood: med. Each team's LocalQueue forwards to the same `training-borrow` (`localqueue-team-example.yaml:26`, README `:1-6`), which has a single `borrowingLimit` and no per-team `nominalQuota`. `withinClusterQueue: LowerPriority` (`:32`) lets a higher-priority job preempt a lower one, but among equal-priority jobs it is first-come: one team submitting a wave of jobs starves the rest. Kueue *attributes* usage per team (`recording-rules.yaml:65-71`) but does not *limit* it — the DoS ref's "per-tenant quotas stop one tenant exhausting shared capacity" is unmet. ADR 0008 (`:27-28`) notes the queue-wide design is intentional but conditional. Fix direction: per-team LocalQueue/ClusterQueue quotas within the cohort, or borrowingLimit-per-flavor sub-splits, before onboarding a second team.

**[RECOVERY] Liveness is loop-alive, not tick-success; a single replica + Recreate + 300s staleness can silently miss a 300s wave-fire window with no standby** — `clusters/pilot/lending/lending-controller.yaml:169-171,:227-235`, `controllers/lending/reconcile.sh:507,:577,:584` / blast: pool / likelihood: med. The heartbeat is touched after *every* tick including handled failures and `schedule_invalid` skips (`:584`, README `:155-166`), so a controller that is alive but failing every kubectl call (blind, API down, RBAC revoked) never restarts and never trips liveness — during the morning reclaim it would silently fail to reclaim, invisible except via the render-latency SLO. Compounding it: `replicas: 1` + `strategy: Recreate` (`:169-171`) with no leader-elected standby (deferred, `:200-203`), and a wave stays due only `WAVE_FIRE_WINDOW_SECONDS` = 300s (`reconcile.sh:507`) while the liveness staleness threshold is also 300s with `failureThreshold: 3` — a wedged controller can take longer to be killed + rescheduled than the wave's fire window, so a wave is missed outright with nothing covering the gap until the window-close mass reclaim. Fix direction: a tick-success signal (metric/readiness) wired to an alert, and bring forward leader election so a standby can take over within a wave window.

**[RESILIENCE] "Reclaim beats the ramp" is schedule-authored, not enforced in code** — `controllers/lending/README.md:81-83`, `clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml:36,:98-102` / blast: pool / likelihood: low. `productionRampAt` (07:00) and `nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds` (600s) are read but **not actuated** by v0 — waves fire at hardcoded `startsAt` times, not at a lead-time computed back from the ramp. The 30-minute margin between the 06:30 close and the 07:00 ramp is a human-authored constant, not a guarantee the controller can defend if a wave is slow or missed. Fix direction: compute wave lead time from the return budget vs the ramp (the documented real-operator scope), and alert if remaining-lent-nodes × return-budget won't clear before ramp.

**[CHANGING LANDSCAPE] Node AMI pinned to `al2023@latest` — scrubbed nodes reboot on a moving target** — `clusters/pilot/karpenter/ec2nodeclass-gpu-held.yaml:14-15`, `ec2nodeclass-web.yaml:8-9` / blast: pool / likelihood: low. The scrub-by-termination model returns "a fresh instance booting a fresh AMI" (`lending-controller.yaml:64-66`); with `@latest` that AMI can silently change under you, so a reclaimed node can come back behaving differently than it left — a latent surprise exactly during the reclaim critical path. The file comment admits it's a scaffold placeholder (`:12-13`). Fix direction: pin the AMI to a digest/version, as the comment already plans.

**[DoS] Inference HPA scales on CPU only for a GPU service; the render endpoint has no app-layer shedding** — `charts/golden-service/templates/hpa.yaml:16-21`, `rehearsal/loadgen.yaml:49-52` / blast: workload / likelihood: low. CPU utilization is a poor proxy for GPU-bound inference load, so the HPA is a weak/mismatched signal; the real render-path protection is warm floor + PriorityClass preemption, which is sound, but there is no rate-limit or load-shedding on the render endpoint itself (the loadgen hits it raw at 4000 RPS). The DoS lens' "autoscaling is not a defense by itself" applies. Fix direction: scale on a GPU/latency custom metric and add request-level shedding at the inference gateway.

## Checked and clean

- **Staged reclaim + non-compounding once-semantics is real.** Waves at 0.25/0.50/1.0 (`schedule.yaml:82-96`); the fired-marker keys on `date + index + startsAt` (`reconcile.sh:515-517`) so game-day re-drives correctly re-fire, and selection excludes already-cordoned nodes (`:521`) as a structural backstop so even a marker-loss re-fire cannot re-count an in-flight reclaim (`:474-484`). Genuinely prevents `1-(1-f)^n` compounding.
- **Malformed schedule fails closed before any kubectl.** `validate_schedule` (`:170-228`) gates schemaVersion, required fields, strict `HH:MM` via `valid_hm` (`:162-166`), and day names; `tick` returns on failure before actuation (`:548`), the loop survives and never crash-loops (`:581`), and `test.sh:217-235` asserts no `action=(tick|taint|reclaim|...)` line ever appears on garbage input. The `valid_hm` gate specifically closes the "arithmetic-error-reads-as-window-closed" mass-untaint bug.
- **Inference/training isolation is strong and layered.** Inference never enters Kueue (`clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml:11-19`); it preempts via PriorityClass `inference-critical` 1000000 (`priorityclasses.yaml:8-16`) while `training-preemptible` is `preemptionPolicy: Never` (`:24-28`) — one-directional. Training tolerates only lendable taints, never warm-floor (`resourceflavors.yaml:16-20,:33-41`), so borrowing cannot overrun the floor. Warm-floor balloon (value -10, instant-preempt, `warm-floor-balloon.yaml:22-29`) plus `budgets: nodes:"0"` (`nodepool-gpu-warm-floor.yaml:50-51`) hold the floor against consolidation.
- **RBAC drift-trap and the offline RBAC-subset check.** The ClusterRole deliberately grants no NodePool/EC2NodeClass verbs (`lending-controller.yaml:44-51`), preventing the whole-pool-replacement drift bug; `test.sh:55-170` parses every `kc` invocation, maps it to (group, resource, verb), and fails closed on anything unmapped — a real "uses only granted verbs" proof runnable without a cluster.
- **The in-cluster-config blind-spot is found and fixed.** `materialize_incluster_kubeconfig` (`:76-107`) documents a live EKS failure where `--request-timeout` disabled kubectl's in-cluster fallback (controller dialed localhost:8080 and went blind) and materializes an explicit token-file kubeconfig; `kc`'s `--request-timeout=30s` (`:74`) bounds every request so a stalled API server can't wedge a tick.
- **Close transition never bare-untaints.** Still-lent nodes route through the same `reclaim_node` path as waves and untaint only on explicitly logged degraded paths (`:301-344`), verified by the close-boundary test (`test.sh:377-398`). The degraded EKS-no-NodeClaim path leaves the node cordoned+parked rather than returning it unscrubbed to inference (`:285-286`) — isolation over availability.
- **Borrower drain is level-triggered and conservative.** Only reactivates Workloads it annotated (`:463-470`, "a Workload a human deactivated is not ours to resurrect" `:443-444`); taint transitions act only on real change (`:322,:327`); event emission is non-fatal (`:129`). Controller image is digest-pinned (`lending-controller.yaml:198`).
- **Game-day rehearses the reclaim shapes and the driver-wedge quarantine.** `scenarios.yaml` covers staged/compressed/storm/failure-injection, and the storm case explicitly asserts the warm floor holds R2 with no stagger (`:96-115`); gates read `*_over_time` windows to survive the stale-during-reclaim gap (`:19-25`).

## Security × reliability tradeoffs observed

- **Idempotency vs crash-resumption (the crown-jewel tension).** The "skip cordoned = in-flight reclaim" rule (`reconcile.sh:330-331,:521`) is exactly what makes re-fires safe *and* what strands a node when the process dies mid-reclaim. The design bought double-act safety at the cost of crash-resume safety; it needs both. Currently the warm floor is the only thing covering the gap.
- **Scrub-safety vs availability, un-timed against the ramp.** The controller correctly prefers isolation (park an unscrubbable node rather than return training's VRAM residue to inference, `:285-286`) — but it does **not** compute whether reclaim beats the 07:00 ramp: `productionRampAt` / `nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds` are read-only (README `:81-83`). So "beat the deadline" is schedule-authored + warm-floor-backstopped, not code-guaranteed. Honest for a v0 skeleton, but the deadline the whole system exists to protect is not enforced by the actuator.
- **GitOps integrity vs incident speed.** Making git "the ONLY write path" (`schedule.yaml:1-5`) is a strong attribution/integrity property and the right default — but it removes the fast, governed emergency lever the changing-landscape lens demands, and the only in-band "stop" (narrow the window) fires an un-staged storm reclaim. The two goals are in genuine tension and currently resolve entirely toward integrity.
- **Simplicity vs tenant isolation.** One shared `training-borrow` queue keeps the model simple and the taints pin training to one pool (ADR 0008 `:27-28`), but it defers all per-tenant DoS protection — acceptable at one pilot team, a gap the moment a second team onboards.</result>

# Infra audit — SRS design lens (2026-07-20)

Read-only design audit of the synorg infra as committed, through the eight lenses
of Google's *Building Secure and Reliable Systems* (least privilege,
understandability, changing landscape, resilience, recovery, DoS, supply chain,
crisis & culture). Findings are grounded in `file:line` and ranked by **blast
radius × likelihood**. Three parallel auditors covered identity/tenancy/supply-chain,
the lending control loop (`reconcile.sh`), and terraform/recovery/crisis.

Scope caveat: cluster RBAC bindings, live GitHub repo settings, and running
secrets were out of scope. Where a claim depends on them, it's flagged.

---

## The headline: the tiered-governance model is convention, not enforcement

Four findings interlock into one meta-finding that dominates the audit. The
`capability-tiers.md` model promises three tiers — *denied at admission*,
*human-by-exception*, *autonomous* — enforced so "none can be merged around."
In the committed repo, **the gate between a PR (human or agent) and a fleet-wide
reconcile is not actually enforced for the two upper tiers:**

- **`capability-tiers.md:24,42-48` claims a "technical gate … proven by repo
  settings (branch protection + path-scoped required reviewers)."** Path-scoped
  required reviewers need a **CODEOWNERS file — there is none** (`find` returns
  nothing; `.github/` holds only workflows), and no ruleset is checked in. The
  load-bearing control for the highest-blast-radius tier (prod topology, quota,
  NodePool changes) is an unversioned, unproven GitHub setting.
- **All five ApplicationSets run `project: default`** (`clusters/mgmt/appsets/regions.yaml:57`,
  `services.yaml:45`, `training-runs.yaml:50`, `observability.yaml:37,109`). The
  built-in `default` AppProject permits all repos, all destinations (`namespace:'*'`,
  `server:'*'`), all resources. The one scoped project (`appproject-example.yaml`)
  is referenced by nothing. Destination namespaces are templated from untrusted
  file content, so a values file naming `kube-system` deploys there unopposed.
- **All AppSets track `HEAD` with `selfHeal:true` + `prune:true`** — a merge on
  the default branch reconciles fleet-wide automatically, with no signed-tag or
  commit-digest pin and no admission-time provenance check.
- **The lending schedule (`schedule.yaml`) — "the ONLY write path" for
  lend/reclaim — rides the same auto-approval lane** (the evidence plane targets
  80% auto-approve, `recording-rules.yaml:114-122`), and `validate_schedule` never
  asserts the pool it aims is actually the lendable pool (see P0-2).

Together: the autonomous and by-exception tiers are a *naming convention over the
change*, not a control that holds when the change is hostile or wrong. Fixing any
one leaves the hole open; they must be closed together — commit CODEOWNERS + the
branch-protection ruleset as code, bind each Application to a per-team AppProject,
and pin `targetRevision` to gated release tags.

---

## P0 — fix before production / before a second tenant

### P0-1 · Tiered governance is unenforced (the meta-finding above)
Blast: **fleet** · Likelihood: **med**. Refs above.

### P0-2 · The warm floor is one auto-approvable typo from deletion
`clusters/pilot/lending/lending-controller.yaml:61-70`, `reconcile.sh:306,375,520`,
`schedule.yaml:43` · Blast: **fleet (the inference SLO floor itself)** ·
Likelihood: **low, consequence catastrophic**.
The controller ClusterRole grants `nodes` patch/update and `nodeclaims` delete
**cluster-wide, unrestricted**. The only thing keeping the full cordon/drain/
delete-NodeClaim machinery off the never-lent warm floor is a code selector
`karpenter.sh/nodepool=$pool`, where `pool` is read straight from the schedule.
Warm floor and lendable differ only by label/taint (same EC2NodeClass, instance
types, ODCR pool). A schedule with `lendablePool: gpu-warm-floor` would drain and
delete the inference floor — and the floor's `budgets: nodes:"0"` + balloon only
stop *Karpenter* consolidation, not an external NodeClaim delete. `validate_schedule`
never checks this. **Fix: pin the selector to a constant or assert
`lendablePool != gpu-warm-floor`, and require human review on `schedule.yaml`.**

### P0-3 · A crash mid-reclaim strands a lent node that both paths then skip
`controllers/lending/reconcile.sh:274-283, 330-331, 521, 517` · Blast:
**pool + workload** · Likelihood: **med**.
`reclaim_node` cordons → drains (up to 360s) → deletes the NodeClaim —
non-atomic. The idempotency guard that makes re-fires safe (`skip cordoned =
"in-flight reclaim"`) is also the trap: if the pod dies during the ~360s drain
(OOM, deploy rollover, node loss), the node returns **cordoned + still lent +
still running training**, and no reconciler resumes it — both paths treat it as
"someone else's." It sits lent past the ramp; only the warm floor saves inference.
**Fix: on each tick, actively re-drive cordoned-lent nodes to completion;
distinguish "live in-flight reclaim" from "abandoned" via a timestamp/annotation.**

### P0-4 · Is the code that runs the code you reviewed? — No.
`charts/training-job/templates/job.yaml:60`, `capability-tiers.md:23`, ADR 0007:16-24,
`clusters/mgmt/appsets/observability.yaml:43,113` · Blast: **region / fleet** ·
Likelihood: **med**.
Workloads resolve as `repository:tag` — a **mutable tag** an agent supplies in a
values file; images are public on ghcr, pulled without auth, with **no cosign /
binary-authorization admission check** anywhere in `policies/`. A "values-only
prod image bump" sits in the **autonomous (auto-merge, no human)** lane. Third-party
Helm charts install fleet-wide on floating minor ranges (`kube-prometheus-stack
65.x`, `dcgm-exporter 3.x` — the file's own TODO admits it). The ghcr push
credential is a **classic PAT with `write:packages`** (ADR 0007:23-24) — long-lived,
can overwrite any tag. **Fix: require digest-pinned images from the canonical
registry via admission policy; verify provenance (cosign) at deploy; pin the
charts; replace the PAT with OIDC/fine-grained token.**

---

## P1 — serious, fix soon

### P1-1 · No fast, governed kill switch; the only in-band "stop" is a storm
`schedule.yaml:1-8`, `reconcile.sh:318-344` · Blast: **fleet** · Likelihood: **med**.
No `lendingEnabled:false` flag, no break-glass. To stop lending under incident you
open a PR (CI + merge + sync — not seconds) or scale the Deployment to 0 (which
*freezes* recovery state). Narrowing the window to force a stop calls `reclaim_node`
on **every** still-lent node in one tick with no `reclaimFraction` staging — the
`storm-all-at-once` case, survived only because the warm floor backstops it.
**Fix: a controller-honored pause flag that halts actuation without freezing
recovery, plus a documented emergency abort.**

### P1-2 · Kyverno `Enforce` gates all pods fleet-wide with no break-glass
`policies/kyverno/tenancy-guard.yaml:16`, `require-team-label.yaml:14`,
`deny-inline-secrets.yaml:16` · Blast: **fleet** · Likelihood: **low-med
(outage-triggered)**.
These match every Pod at `validationFailureAction: Enforce` via the Kyverno
webhook (default `failurePolicy: Fail`). A Kyverno outage fails closed and blocks
*all* pod creation fleet-wide — no cached fallback or documented bypass exists.
(Contrast the in-process VAP, which is materially safer — see Clean.) **Fix:
document a scoped `failurePolicy` carve-out / webhook-bypass runbook and alert on
Kyverno availability.**

### P1-3 · Live ODCR apply runs on local terraform state
`runbooks/deploy-platform.md:57`, contra `infra/terraform/backend.tf.example:39-41`
· Blast: **region + state trust-root** · Likelihood: **med**.
The bootstrap for the single most irreversible module runs `terraform init
-backend=false` (comment says "wire real backend for a live apply" — the flag does
the opposite), so the apply that creates `prevent_destroy` + `unlimited`-duration
held reservations writes **local** state — no lock, no versioning, no shared
truth. A lost state file orphans irreversible capacity. **Fix: init against the
real S3+Dynamo backend before any capacity-touching apply; delete the
`-backend=false` line.**

### P1-4 · No per-tenant Kueue quota — one team starves the borrow queue
`clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml:41-60`,
`localqueue-team-example.yaml:26` · Blast: **workload (other training tenants)** ·
Likelihood: **med**.
Every team's LocalQueue forwards to one `training-borrow` ClusterQueue with a
single `borrowingLimit` and no per-team `nominalQuota`. Among equal-priority jobs
it's first-come — one team's wave starves the rest. Usage is *attributed* per team
but not *limited*. Acceptable at one pilot team; a gap the moment a second onboards
(ADR 0008 notes the design is intentional-but-conditional). **Fix: per-team quotas
within the cohort before the second tenant.**

### P1-5 · Liveness is loop-alive, not tick-success; a single replica can miss a wave silently
`lending-controller.yaml:169-171`, `reconcile.sh:584,507` · Blast: **pool** ·
Likelihood: **med**.
The heartbeat is touched after *every* tick including handled failures, so a
controller that is alive but failing every kubectl call (API down, RBAC revoked)
never trips liveness — it silently fails to reclaim during the morning window.
`replicas:1` + `Recreate` + no leader-elected standby, and the wave fire window
(300s) equals the liveness staleness threshold — a wedged controller can take
longer to be killed + rescheduled than the window, so a wave is missed outright.
**Fix: a tick-success signal wired to an alert; bring forward leader election.**

### P1-6 · Break-glass is pre-authorized but not audited
`runbooks/training-intake-break-glass.md:12-29`, `runbooks/operations.md:127,140`,
ADR 0004:16-21 · Blast: **fleet / region** · Likelihood: **med**.
The break-glass paths are well pre-authorized by condition and well-bounded, but
nothing on the path requires **recording the invocation** — no event, no ledger
row, no auto-opened review. `operations.md:140` itself notes hand-applying "breaks
audit," yet the sanctioned bypasses emit no record of who/when/why. Pre-authorized
without audited is half the control. **Fix: every break-glass action writes a
timestamped who/why/what record and auto-opens a review.**

### P1-7 · Suspected-compromise recovery destroys forensic evidence
`runbooks/operations.md:33,116-120` · Blast: **cloud-account** · Likelihood: **low**.
"Rebuild the hub from Terraform + git (it is fully reproducible)" is a fast, clean
*availability* recovery — but on a **security** incident it wipes the running
state needed to learn how the breach happened. No "preserve evidence before you
rebuild/rotate" step exists. **Fix: an evidence-preservation gate (snapshot EBS,
export audit logs, isolate) on the compromise path, distinct from the plain-outage
rebuild.**

### P1-8 · No incident-command structure or blameless posture defined
`runbooks/gpu-node-quarantine.md:91`, `node-scrub.md:111`, `capacity-carve.md:76`
· Blast: **org process** · Likelihood: **med**.
Four runbooks say "open an incident" but nothing defines **who runs it**: no
Incident Commander, no comms/scribe split, no severity ladder, no postmortem
template, no blameless framing. The game-day "signed-off report" is a gate
artifact, not a retrospective. **Fix: an incident-response runbook defining
IC/comms/scribe, escalation, and a blameless postmortem template.**

---

## P2 — hardening

- **P2-1 · Checkpoint store uses the AWS-managed KMS key with no bucket policy** —
  `checkpoint-store/main.tf:43-48`. `aws:kms` with no `kms_master_key_id` → the
  unscopable `aws/s3` managed key; access gated by IRSA only. Training state is an
  untrusted trust domain vs customer-data inference. Fix: customer-managed CMK with
  a key policy scoped to trainer IRSA + a deny-by-default bucket policy (TLS + CMK).
- **P2-2 · Training trainer container has no `securityContext` (may run as root) on
  shared lendable nodes** — `charts/training-job/templates/job.yaml:58-116`
  (contrast the hardened controller Dockerfile). Fix: hardened pod/container
  securityContext.
- **P2-3 · `lendable-networkpolicy` ships a `10.0.0.0/8` egress allow placeholder**
  — `policies/kyverno/lendable-networkpolicy.yaml:56-61`. If an overlay forgets to
  pin the CIDR, training pods reach all of RFC1918. Fix: leave it unset/invalid so
  it fails loudly, or template as a required overlay value.
- **P2-4 · "Reclaim beats the ramp" is schedule-authored, not code-enforced** —
  `README.md:81-83`, `schedule.yaml:36`. `productionRampAt` /
  `nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds` are read-only in v0; the 30-min margin is a
  human constant. Fix: compute wave lead time from the return budget vs ramp; alert
  if remaining-lent × return-budget won't clear.
- **P2-5 · CI `validate`/`integration`/`nightly` set no `permissions:` block and
  fetch toolchains without checksum; actions pinned to mutable major tags** —
  contrast the model `e2e.yaml`. Fix: `permissions: contents: read`, verify tool
  sha256, pin actions to SHAs.
- **P2-6 · Spoke API server default admits the whole VPC `/16`** —
  `regions/pilot/main.tf:29-38`, `variables.tf:85-89`. `hub_ingress_cidr` defaults
  to `10.42.0.0/16`. Fix: make it required (no default) so a prod apply must state
  the real narrow CIDR.
- **P2-7 · Node AMI pinned to `al2023@latest`** — `ec2nodeclass-gpu-held.yaml:14-15`.
  Scrubbed nodes reboot on a moving target on the reclaim critical path. Fix: pin to
  a digest/version (comment already plans it).
- **P2-8 · Inference HPA scales on CPU only for a GPU service; no app-layer
  shedding** — `charts/golden-service/templates/hpa.yaml:16-21`. Fix: scale on a
  GPU/latency metric; add request-level shedding at the inference gateway.
- **P2-9 · Two tenancy policies overclaim what they enforce** —
  `eso-namespace-scope.yaml:65-68` checks `store.name == namespace` (over-denies
  valid names, doesn't verify ownership); `deny-cross-namespace-refs.yaml:42-56` is
  a naming lint, not the cross-tenant secret control the docs imply (k8s already
  resolves secret refs namespace-locally). Fine as defense-in-depth; relabel the
  docs so the stated invariant matches the CEL.
- **P2-10 · Checkpoint lifecycle omits incomplete-multipart-upload cleanup** —
  `checkpoint-store/main.tf:63-76`. Multi-GB shards killed mid-flush bill
  indefinitely. Fix: `abort_incomplete_multipart_upload { days_after_initiation = 1 }`.
- **P2-11 · Terraform state-bucket write access is unscoped in the shipped config**
  — `backend.tf.example:24-45`. State is the trust root; the example sets
  encrypt+lock+versioning but no bucket policy / MFA-delete / least-privilege deploy
  role. Fix: ship a state-bucket policy + role guidance alongside the example.

---

## Checked and clean (what's genuinely solid)

The audit found real strength — this is a well-built skeleton, not a shaky one:

- **Staged reclaim with non-compounding once-semantics is real** — waves at
  0.25/0.50/1.0, the fired-marker keys on date+index+startsAt so game-day re-drives
  re-fire correctly, and selection excludes already-cordoned nodes as a structural
  backstop (`reconcile.sh:515-521,474-484`). Genuinely prevents `1-(1-f)^n`.
- **Malformed schedule fails closed before any kubectl** — `validate_schedule`
  gates schema/fields/`HH:MM`/day-names; `test.sh:217-235` asserts no action line
  ever appears on garbage input. The `valid_hm` gate specifically kills the
  "arithmetic-error-reads-as-window-closed" mass-untaint bug.
- **Inference/training isolation is strong and one-directional** — inference never
  enters Kueue and preempts via PriorityClass 1000000; `training-preemptible` is
  `preemptionPolicy: Never`; training tolerates only lendable taints, never
  warm-floor; warm-floor balloon + `budgets: nodes:"0"` hold the floor against
  consolidation.
- **The offline RBAC-subset proof** — `test.sh:55-170` parses every kubectl call,
  maps it to (group,resource,verb), and fails closed on anything the ClusterRole
  doesn't grant — a real least-privilege proof runnable without a cluster. The role
  deliberately grants no NodePool/EC2NodeClass verbs (prevents whole-pool-replacement
  drift).
- **The in-cluster-config blind-spot is found and fixed in code** —
  `materialize_incluster_kubeconfig` documents a live EKS failure (a
  `--request-timeout` disabling kubectl's in-cluster fallback → controller went
  blind) and every request is bounded by `--request-timeout=30s`.
- **`e2e.yaml` is a model least-privilege CI surface** — `workflow_dispatch` only,
  double-confirm gate, OIDC (no static keys), an assume-role that explicitly
  *excludes* `ec2:CancelCapacityReservation`, teardown in `always()`. `validate.yaml`
  uses `pull_request` (not `pull_request_target`) so fork code never runs with
  secrets.
- **ArgoCD hub hardened** — `exec.enabled:false`, `server.insecure:false`, Dex
  disabled, `Prune=false` so a bad sync can't self-delete the hub. Controller image
  digest-pinned.
- **The `deny-cross-namespace-refs` VAP is an in-process, network-independent
  fail-closed** — materially safer than the Kyverno webhook (only a CEL bug, not an
  outage, can trip it).
- **ODCR blast-radius discipline is excellent** — `prevent_destroy` on held
  reservations, positive-count validation, a verify-before-terminate gate enforced
  one-instance-per-invocation. The one source-tree IAM `"*"` is on
  `ec2:DescribeCapacityReservations` (which AWS can't scope); the consumption
  actions are ARN-scoped. No hardcoded secrets/account-IDs/ARNs in source. Providers
  hash-locked in all four roots. State backend example is encrypt+lock+versioned.
- **game-day is a real rehearsal** — real preemption, repeat-runs before any
  verdict, abort-on-unmeasurable-evidence, cross-run variance gate. The strangler
  migration is incremental with a cheap weight-flip rollback.

---

## Security × reliability tradeoffs (named, per the SRS method)

- **Idempotency vs crash-resumption (the crown-jewel tension).** The "skip cordoned
  = in-flight reclaim" rule is what makes re-fires safe *and* what strands a node
  when the process dies mid-reclaim (P0-3). The design bought double-act safety at
  the cost of crash-resume safety; it needs both. The warm floor currently covers
  the gap.
- **GitOps integrity vs incident speed.** "Git is the ONLY write path" is a strong
  attribution property and the right default — but it removes the fast emergency
  lever, and the only in-band "stop" fires an un-staged storm (P1-1). Currently
  resolves entirely toward integrity; needs a governed-*and*-fast pause.
- **Scrub-safety vs availability, un-timed against the ramp.** The controller
  correctly parks an unscrubbable node rather than return training's VRAM residue to
  inference (isolation over availability) — but it does not *compute* whether reclaim
  beats the ramp (P2-4). The deadline the whole system exists to protect is
  warm-floor-backstopped, not actuator-guaranteed. Honest for a v0 skeleton.
- **Fail-closed admission vs availability.** Kyverno `Enforce` fleet-wide with no
  break-glass (P1-2) trades a tenancy breach (unrecoverable) against an outage
  (recoverable) — the right direction, but missing the cached-last-known-good /
  bypass that keeps an engine blip from becoming an outage.

The whole value case asserts the double-pay (separate prod + R&D GPU pools) as a given and never says **why** they're separate. That's the first thing a skeptic asks, and the answer is exactly what justifies synorg over "just merge them."

## Why prod & R&D GPUs are separate in the real world
Ranked by how well each justifies synorg (strongest → weakest):

1. **Trust / tenancy boundary — THE load-bearing reason.** R&D runs arbitrary, experimental, often-privileged code (notebooks, unvetted deps). Prod inference serves *customer data* under SLA + compliance (SOC2/HIPAA/PCI). Put R&D's code on the same GPU that just held customer inference and you have data-residue in VRAM, blast-radius, and a compliance violation. This is precisely what synorg's **scrub-on-return** (discard the instance, GPU memory never survives the boundary) exists to handle. The boundary is real; synorg makes crossing it *safe*.
2. **Reliability posture — structural.** Inference needs stable, warm, reserved capacity (the insurance floor). Training tolerates preemption/spot/checkpointing. Different capacity strategies → naturally different pools.
3. **Workload shape / hardware — structural.** Inference: latency-sensitive, spiky, fewer GPUs, modest interconnect. Training: throughput, big NVLink/EFA clusters (p5.48xl 8×H100), checkpointable batch. Different instance families, different Karpenter pools.
4. **Org / budget silos — the WEAK one.** Separate cost centers, procurement, approval chains. This is the only reason that's *not* structural — and the one a CFO weaponizes: *"then just merge the budgets and share one pool."*

## The grill point
The doc must prove the separation is **(1)+(2)+(3)** — structural — not **(4)** — an accounting artifact. Because:
- If a reader believes it's (4), the fix is "pool the budget + one Kueue cluster," and **synorg is over-build, dead on arrival.**
- The *tell* that it's (1): synorg's entire scrub/lend/reclaim machinery is **only** justified if the trust boundary is real. If it weren't, you'd pool naively with Kueue and skip the lending controller. So synorg's own existence is the proof the boundary is structural.

This is why §7's *"why not just Karpenter+Kueue without lending"* answer is too shallow — it says "nothing makes lend→reclaim safe against the SLO" but never names *why the boundary you're crossing is there*. The real answer: **the clusters are separate because naive pooling breaks tenancy AND reliability; synorg is the safe bridge, not the merge.**

Make the trust boundary the hero (it's what scrub-on-return proves). *(My rec — this is arguably a prerequisite to the whole doc.)*

- **(c)** You think the separation is *partly* (4)/org — in which case the honest doc must concede synorg only wins where the boundary is structural, and say so.

Q3 waiting: how the doc handles "why separate / why not pool" — **(a)** dedicated §0 with trust-boundary as hero *(my rec)*, **(b)** one §7 bullet, **(c)** concede synorg only wins where the boundary is structural. Plus: is my 4-reason ranking complete, or is there a real-world driver I missed?
a cant shrink, but also the q is - why rnd and prod are using different gpu clusters in the real world? and if there is a way to solve contradictions in any  other way than separating the?

Yes — you've just named the deepest cut. "Separate clusters" isn't a fact, it's a **contradiction resolution**, and it's only one of several. In TRIZ terms this is a physical contradiction:

> The same GPU capacity must be **dedicated to prod** (hard latency SLA + trust isolation) **AND used by R&D** (or you double-pay for idle).

"Separate clusters" resolves it by **separation in space**. That's the expensive resolution. Here's the full menu — and where synorg actually sits:

## The resolution menu
| # | Resolution | What it is | Who does it |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Space** | separate clusters | prod GPUs ≠ R&D GPUs | the status quo synorg attacks (double-pay) |
| **Time** | same GPU, prod by day / train by night | scheduled lend + reclaim-before-ramp | **synorg's core move** |
| **Condition** | inference always wins, training backfills, evicted on demand | Kueue priority-preemption, no time-windows | the "why not just Kueue" alternative |
| **System / silicon** | partition one GPU into isolated slices, simultaneous | **MIG** (H100 → 7 hardware-isolated instances) | the alternative a technical HoP *will* raise |
| **Market** | don't own the contradiction — rent pooled capacity | neocloud / broker pools across many tenants | the alternative a CFO will raise |

## The insight the doc is missing
synorg picks **time-separation** — but the sharp question is *why not the simpler **condition** (pure preemption)?* Training backfills, inference preempts it whenever it wants — no schedule, no windows. Simpler. Why doesn't synorg do that?

**Because preemption isn't instant across the trust boundary.** A training job can't be evicted in milliseconds — it must checkpoint, drain, and critically the GPU VRAM must be **scrubbed** before inference can safely reuse it (that's the whole R9 tenancy rule). So "inference needs the GPU *now*" can't be served reactively — there's a drain+scrub latency. That's *exactly* why synorg reclaims **ahead of predicted demand** (before the morning ramp) instead of reacting to it, and keeps a **warm floor** (spatial separation) for the unpredictable spikes.

So synorg is really a **hybrid: time-separation for the predictable overnight idle + space-separation (warm floor) for the SLA-critical unpredictable demand.** The warm floor isn't a bolt-on — it's the half of the design that covers what time-separation structurally can't. That's the actual design thesis, and the doc never states it.

## The three preconditions (this is the honest boundary)
synorg is the right resolution **only where all three hold**:
1. **GPU is scarce** — can't buy on-demand (2026: confirmed) → space-separation via "just buy more" is off the table.
2. **The trust+SLA boundary is real** → naive pooling (one Kueue cluster) violates tenancy/compliance.
3. **Scrub+checkpoint latency is nonzero** → you can't preempt *reactively* fast enough for a hard latency SLA, so you must reclaim *ahead* of demand.

**Remove any one and a simpler resolution wins:** GPU abundant → buy per-use. Boundary soft → pool with Kueue preemption. Scrub instant (MIG hardware isolation matures) → simultaneous space-separation-in-silicon, no lending machine at all. **MIG and neoclouds are synorg's real competitors — not the pure-spot / buy-more / do-nothing strawmen in §7.**

## Grill finding
The doc asserts separation as given and lists weak "why not just…" rebuttals. It should instead **frame synorg as a chosen contradiction-resolution** — "we resolve dedicate-vs-utilize by time-separation + a warm-floor, because condition-preemption can't beat scrub latency against a hard SLA" — and name the **real** alternatives (MIG, neocloud) with the three preconditions that decide when synorg wins vs when it loses. That's a far stronger, more honest §7 than the current strawmen, and it pre-empts the two objections a real technical+finance room actually raises.

---

**Q3 (updated) — how to handle "why separate / other resolutions":**
- **(a)** §0 "why separate = structural boundary" *(my earlier rec)* — **now upgrade it:** make it "the contradiction and why time-separation resolves it," fold in the three preconditions, and replace the §7 strawmen with MIG + neocloud. *(My rec.)*
- **(b)** lighter — just swap §7's strawmen for the real alternatives (MIG/neocloud), skip the full contradiction framing.
- **(c)** you think one of the three preconditions is shaky (e.g. MIG already good enough that precondition 3 is failing) — which would genuinely threaten synorg, and the doc should say so.

# Lending schedule — the ONLY write path for lending intent (KTD5: capacity
# intent lives in git; scarcity is evidence, not an error). A PR editing the
# `data.schedule.yaml` document below is the sole way to change when GPU nodes
# are lent, how far the training borrow curve is allowed to grow, and how the
# morning reclaim is staged. Nothing else — no imperative kubectl, no console —
# moves lending state; the U8 lifecycle controller only reads this file and
# actuates from it (region-local, so it survives a hub/ArgoCD outage: once this
# ConfigMap is applied the controller keeps running the schedule locally).
#
# Delivered as a ConfigMap so it is a first-class, schema-checked cluster object
# and mounts straight into the controller (see lending-controller.yaml). The
# schedule body is a block scalar the controller parses as YAML; the `#` field
# comments are ordinary YAML comments and are ignored by the parser.
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: lending-schedule
  namespace: lending
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: lending-controller
    app.kubernetes.io/component: schedule
data:
  schedule.yaml: |
    # ---- Lending schedule (git-controlled intent) --------------------------
    # schemaVersion: bump when fields change so the controller can reject a
    # schedule it does not understand rather than mis-actuate on it.
    schemaVersion: 1

    # timezone: all clock times below are region-local and DST-aware. The pilot
    # region (eu-west-1) is Europe/Dublin; off-peak is defined in local time so
    # the window tracks local demand, not UTC.
    timezone: Europe/Dublin

    # productionRampAt: the render-demand ramp the reclaim must finish before.
    # Every reclaim wave lead time is measured back from this instant.
    productionRampAt: "07:00"

    # targets: the conventions.md pinned names this schedule actuates against.
    # The controller patches Node taints and the Kueue ClusterQueue borrowingLimit
    # for exactly these objects — never the NodePool spec.template (drift trap,
    # see lending-controller.yaml RBAC comment).
    targets:
      lendablePool: gpu-lendable                       # Karpenter NodePool name (never edited, only its Nodes tainted)
      lentTaint: "lending.synorg.io/lent=true:NoSchedule"  # added to Node objects while lent; removed on return-to-prod
      trainingQueue: training-borrow                   # Kueue ClusterQueue whose borrowingLimit this curve drives (U6 owns the file)

    # windows: when the lendable pool may carry training. Opening the window
    # flips the lent taint ON (training tolerates it, inference does not);
    # closing it is the deadline by which the reclaim waves must have returned
    # every lent node to the prod-tolerable state.
    windows:
      - name: nightly-offpeak
        opensAt: "22:00"    # window opens; lending.synorg.io/lent flips ON across lendable Nodes
        closesAt: "06:30"   # last instant a node may still be lent; final wave completes here
        days: ["Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"]

    # borrowingLimitCurve: the Kueue `training-borrow` ClusterQueue borrowingLimit,
    # expressed as a percentage of lendable GPU capacity, stepping DOWN ahead of
    # the ramp. Shrinking the borrow limit stops Kueue admitting *new* borrow
    # before kube-scheduler PriorityClass preemption is needed — the render path
    # never queues (KTD6). The controller converts gpuLimitPct → an absolute
    # nvidia.com/gpu quantity against current lendable capacity and patches the
    # ClusterQueue borrowingLimit; kube-scheduler preemption is only the
    # emergency layer if demand outruns this curve.
    borrowingLimitCurve:
      - at: "22:00"
        gpuLimitPct: 100    # full borrow allowed once the window opens
      - at: "05:30"
        gpuLimitPct: 75     # first cut, aligned to reclaim wave-1
      - at: "06:00"
        gpuLimitPct: 50     # aligned to wave-2
      - at: "06:30"
        gpuLimitPct: 0      # no borrow past the window close; aligned to wave-3

    # reclaimWaves: staged drain-and-return of lent nodes ahead of the ramp
    # (KTD12: staged waves so the warm floor plus stagger protect R2, never a
    # single storm). Each wave shrinks quota (via the curve above) then drains
    # and returns a fraction of the still-lent nodes. Preferring pre-scrubbed
    # nodes keeps the reimage off the morning critical path (TRIZ preliminary
    # action; see nightScrubRotation).
    reclaimWaves:
      - name: wave-1-early
        startsAt: "05:30"
        reclaimFraction: 0.25   # fraction of currently-lent nodes drained + returned this wave
        drainGraceSeconds: 120  # KTD12 training-eviction grace; matches the 5-min checkpoint cadence
        preferPreScrubbed: true # take night-scrubbed nodes first so return does not wait on reimage
      - name: wave-2-mid
        startsAt: "06:00"
        reclaimFraction: 0.50
        drainGraceSeconds: 120
        preferPreScrubbed: true
      - name: wave-3-final
        startsAt: "06:30"
        reclaimFraction: 1.0    # all remaining lent nodes returned before productionRampAt
        drainGraceSeconds: 120
        preferPreScrubbed: true

    # nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds: the measured p95 reimage → prod-ready
    # budget (KTD12). Pinned here from U8 evidence; it is the wave lead-time
    # input — each wave's startsAt sits at least this far ahead of when its
    # nodes must be serving. Revisit when U9 telemetry moves the measured p95.
    nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds: 600

    # nightScrubRotation: rotate node scrub through the night where training
    # backlog allows, so reclaim waves draw from a pool of already-fresh nodes
    # and the morning ramp never blocks on a reimage (TRIZ: scrub off the
    # critical path, adopted U8). true = controller may pre-scrub idle lent
    # nodes mid-window; false = scrub only at reclaim time.
    nightScrubRotation: true

content.text:
Grounded in `schedule.yaml` — here's the actual mechanism, and it answers your question by revealing synorg **doesn't** solve daytime contention; it sidesteps it.

## During the day: no contention, by design
The lend window is **22:00–06:30 only** (`windows.nightly-offpeak`). Outside it:
- The `lending.synorg.io/lent` taint is **OFF** → the lendable pool is prod-tolerable.
- `borrowingLimitCurve` is at **0%** (drops 100→75→50→0 from 05:30→06:30) → Kueue admits **zero** new training borrow during the day.

So **all day, the lendable pool is prod's.** Training is simply *not scheduled onto it* between 06:30 and 22:00. Prod inference runs on lendable + warm floor; R&D gets **nothing** from the lendable pool during the day. There's no daytime prod-vs-R&D fight because R&D isn't admitted there in daylight.

## What that means (the part the doc under-states)
synorg **only monetizes the overnight idle.** It kills the *overnight slice* of the double-pay — not all of it. Concretely:
- **R&D's daytime GPU demand is out of scope.** If a researcher needs a GPU at 2pm, synorg gives them nothing — they run on R&D's own residual dedicated capacity, or **queue for 22:00.**
- The whole thing rests on a **time-asymmetry assumption**: inference is *diurnal* (busy day / idle night) **and** training is *time-flexible* (batch, tolerates "runs tonight"). synorg recovers only the **overlap** between inference's idle hours and R&D's time-shiftable backlog.

## Why this is the sharpest version of the "R&D backlog alignment" risk
The doc already flags it in §2 — *"the 70% number assumes R&D always has overnight work to fill 35 lent GPUs… if the backlog is thin, bursty, or deadline-misaligned with the window, utilization craters"* — but buries it as a caveat. Your question exposes it as a **mechanism-level boundary**, not a footnote:

> synorg resolves the **night overlap** by time-separation. It does **not** resolve **simultaneous daytime demand** — that case still needs a separate resolution (R&D's own daytime GPUs, or MIG, or the job waits). Time-separation can only recover capacity during the hours the two demands *don't* overlap.

A team doing interactive daytime experimentation gets little. A team running batch training that's happy to land overnight gets the full value. **The recoverable value is entirely a property of R&D's work rhythm** — which the doc says is "a property of *your* R&D org, not the platform," but never connects to the *daytime = zero* mechanism.

## Grill finding (fix-list)
Add to the value case (probably the §0 mechanism section): **"What synorg does NOT do — daytime R&D demand."** State plainly that lending recovers the overnight idle only; daytime simultaneous demand is out of scope and still separately provisioned; the recoverable value = overlap(inference-idle-hours, R&D-time-flexible-backlog). This *strengthens* credibility (it's the honest boundary) and pre-empts the exact "what about when both need it at once?" question you just asked — which a Head of Platform will ask in the first five minutes.

---

Does this match your mental model, or did you expect synorg to give R&D **daytime** capacity too (i.e., lend during daytime inference *troughs*, not just overnight)? Because that's a real design fork:
- **Current design:** overnight-only lend window.
- **Alternative:** dynamic lending on *any* measured inference trough (incl. daytime dips) — more recovery, but far more reclaim churn and scrub cycles, and much tighter SLA risk (daytime reclaim races live peak demand).

Want that daytime-trough option on the grill as a design question, or is overnight-only the deliberate, settled choice?

Sharp — you've hit the actual load-bearing logic. Yes: if we hold the HW 24/7, there IS daytime capacity sitting there. But **daytime idle and overnight idle are different kinds of idle**, and that difference is the whole design.

## Held capacity splits three ways, not two
At any moment the held fleet is one of:
1. **Actively serving inference** — during the day this is most of it (the fleet is *sized for daytime peak* — that's its job).
2. **Surge headroom you must keep** — the warm floor. Held idle *on purpose* as insurance, because a spike can't be met by buying on demand (2026 evidence). This idle is **not recoverable — it's the product.**
3. **Safely-lendable idle** — capacity that's idle *and* will stay idle long enough to reclaim + scrub ahead of demand.

Your question is really: *"isn't there bucket-3 during the day too?"*

## Why daytime idle mostly isn't bucket-3
Daytime idle is **spike-prone and short-runway.** Lend it and a business-hours inference spike forces a *reactive* reclaim against live demand — and scrub latency (you must discard the instance before inference reuses the GPU) means you **can't reclaim fast enough** to protect a hard latency SLA. So daytime idle is unsafe to lend. It's therefore either:
- **bucket-2 (insurance)** — headroom you're keeping for the spike, or
- **oversizing** — genuine excess → that's a **shrink signal**, not a lending opportunity.

**This is the key move:** daytime idle is a *shrink-or-insurance* question, not a *lending* question. Overnight idle is the *lending* target because it's predictable and long-runway — hours before the morning ramp, so you reclaim **ahead** of demand, not reactively.

## So your logic, completed
"We hold the HW, so R&D could run daytime" → R&D running daytime = contending with **live inference** on the same GPUs = the unsafe condition-preemption case (constant preemption + scrub-latency SLA risk). That's *exactly why* R&D holds separate daytime capacity, and why synorg lends only the **safe** (overnight) idle. Holding the HW doesn't make daytime capacity *free to lend* — during the day it's either working, insuring, or oversized.

And the punchline that ties back to the value case: **if the pilot's `utilization-of-held` shows big persistent daytime idle, that's not "lend more" — it's "the book is too big, shrink it."** The measurement is what tells insurance from oversizing. That's the de-risking value from Q2, made concrete.

## The honest exception (your instinct isn't wrong)
*Predictable* daytime troughs — a single-region service with a timezone gap, a nightly-batch customer, a lunch dip — **are** safely lendable (predictable = reclaimable ahead). Overnight-only leaves those unclaimed. So "overnight-only" is the biggest *safe* block, not the *only* one — the daytime-predictable-trough lending is a real roadmap increment, gated on the measurement proving the trough is predictable enough.

## Grill finding (fix-list)
The value case must state the **three-bucket split** explicitly: held capacity is serving / insurance / safely-lendable, and **daytime idle is a shrink-or-insurance signal, not a lending target** — because the smart reader (you, in three questions) immediately asks "why not use the daytime idle?" Answering it converts the biggest apparent hole into the clearest statement of what synorg is: *it lends only the idle it can safely reclaim ahead of demand, and measures the rest so you can shrink it.*

content.text:
Confirmed at `priorityclasses.yaml`: the platform models **exactly two** classes — `inference-critical` (1000000) and `training-preemptible` (1000). Nothing between. So both the value case *and* the platform assume a binary. You just named the thing they're missing, and it's the deepest finding of this whole grill.

## Three workloads, two axes that matter
| Workload | SLA | Trust domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Realtime inference** (interactive) | hard (ms), spiky, diurnal | customer data |
| **Batch inference** (text-to-video) | soft (deadline mins–hrs), revenue | **customer data — SAME as realtime** |
| **R&D training** | softest (best-effort, internal) | **different — needs scrub** |

The two axes — SLA hardness *and* trust domain — don't move together. That's the whole point.

## The consequence that breaks the binary model
**Batch inference is the same trust domain as realtime.** So handing a node from batch-inference back to realtime needs **no scrub** — just checkpoint + requeue, fast. Which means:

- **Daytime troughs → batch inference.** Same domain, no scrub, fast preempt → *safe to soak daytime idle.* No lending layer needed — this is plain Kueue/kube-scheduler priority. This is the daytime idle you've been circling for three turns: it's not lost, it's **batch-inference's to fill.**
- **Overnight idle → R&D training.** Cross-domain, scrub required, slow reclaim → only safe with long runway → synorg's lending machinery, overnight only.

Realtime sits on top of both and preempts either.

## What this does to the economics (the big one)
The value case prices recovered idle at the **training-substitute price ($1.5–2.5/GPU-hr)** — the *lowest-value* filler. But the idle fills as a **priority stack by value**:

1. Realtime peaks (top)
2. **Batch inference** — priced at *customer revenue* (or avoided batch-capacity spend), and it's **endogenous, certain demand** if you have a t2v product — not the speculative R&D backlog §2 flags as "the single largest swing factor."
3. R&D training — the residual, priced at training-substitute.

**So the current value case describes only step 3** — the smallest, hardest, most-speculative slice — and omits steps 1–2 entirely. If batch inference demand is real, synorg's recoverable value is *materially larger and far more certain* than the 2–6% the doc computes, because you'd price the idle at batch-inference revenue, not training substitution.

## The product reframe
synorg isn't "lend inference GPUs to R&D overnight." It's a **SLA-tiered, trust-aware capacity fabric:**
1. **Soak idle with your own batch inference first** — same domain, just priorities, no scrub, daytime *and* overnight, revenue-priced. Cheapest and biggest.
2. **Lend the scrub-bounded residual to R&D overnight** — the cross-domain part that actually needs synorg's lending controller, ledger, and scrub.

**Lending is the last mile, not the whole story.** The novel, expensive machinery (scrub, staged reclaim, zero-net-release) is *only* required for the R&D cross-domain slice — the README currently over-applies "scrub every reclaim" to a batch-inference case that never needs it.

## Grill finding (the biggest fix)
- **Value case**: model three workloads; price recovered idle at the priority-stack (batch-inference revenue on top); lead with batch inference as the certain filler, R&D training as the residual.
- **Platform**: needs a **third priority class** — batch-inference, value between 1000 and 1000000, preemptible, same-trust-domain (no scrub). Today's two-tier stack can't express it. Real design increment, not a doc edit.

---

**The gating question — is batch inference actually a workload for the target?** You gave t2v as "say," so:

- **(a)** Yes, real and significant (t2v / async generation is a core product) → this is the **headline reframe**: batch inference is the primary idle-filler, R&D lending is the residual, and both value case *and* platform need the third tier. *(If true, this is bigger than everything else in the grill.)*
- **(b)** Maybe / small → footnote it: note the three-class model and that batch inference could soak daytime idle, but keep R&D-overnight as the pilot's headline.
- **(c)** No — the target is realtime-only inference + R&D → the binary stands, and this whole branch is out of scope.

Which is it — and if (a), does synorg's own product actually have batch inference today, or is this the target *customer's* workload mix?
